content:
Step-size ideas predate deep learning: **Cauchy** (1847) proposed steepest descent with manually chosen step lengths, and **Armijo** (1966) formalized **line search** rules that pick η each iteration to guarantee sufficient decrease—showing practitioners always needed a mechanism to control step magnitude.

Classical convex optimization literature analyzed convergence rates for gradient methods with fixed and diminishing step sizes (e.g. **Robbins–Monro** stochastic approximation, 1951, uses decreasing η sequences for noisy gradient estimates).

Neural network training in the 1980s–90s relied heavily on hand-tuned constant learning rates; **LeCun** and colleagues popularized practical heuristics tying η to batch size and weight initialization.

Modern deep learning revived interest in **schedules**—step decay, exponential decay, **warmup**, and **cosine annealing**—as models and datasets scaled. Adaptive optimizers (**AdaGrad**, **RMSprop**, **Adam**, 2010s) added per-parameter scaling while retaining a global base η.

Today η remains the first hyperparameter engineers sweep, even as schedulers and adaptive methods automate much of the old manual trial-and-error.

timeline.label: Steepest descent and line search
timeline.description: Classical optimizers choose or search for a step size each iteration instead of assuming a fixed stride works everywhere.

timeline.label: Robbins–Monro SGD
timeline.description: Stochastic approximation theory uses diminishing step-size sequences to guarantee convergence with noisy gradients.

timeline.label: Deep learning schedules
timeline.description: Large-scale training popularizes step decay, warmup, and cosine schedules alongside framework support for lr schedulers.
